questions.prompt: Key difference between Tailwind utilities and inline styles?

choices.text: Inline styles can't do hover, focus, dark mode, media queries, group/peer states; can't compose into a design system; have higher specificity that breaks cascade. Tailwind utilities are CSS classes from a config — support pseudo-classes, media queries, animations, AND enforce a design system scale. You can escape via arbitrary values, but the friction nudges toward consistency.

questions.explanation: Utilities support pseudo-classes/media/dark AND enforce design-system scale. Inline does neither.

choices.text: Tailwind's JIT scans source files for FULL class strings at build time; it can't see partial template-literal constructions. Fix: use complete class names in your conditional logic so Tailwind sees them statically. `const colorClass = color === 'red' ? 'bg-red-500' : 'bg-blue-500'`. For truly dynamic combinations, add classes to the `safelist` in tailwind.config.

questions.explanation: JIT scans for full class strings statically. Use complete classnames in logic; or safelist.

questions.prompt: Same Tailwind class string copied 5+ times. Right abstraction?

choices.text: Extract a React/Vue/Svelte COMPONENT. Components are the Tailwind reuse unit — class-variance-authority or conditional classes handle variants cleanly; refactors stay in one file; types flow. `@apply` is officially discouraged for app UI by Tailwind itself: brings back CSS naming, specificity issues, variant explosion, scattered refactoring. @apply has narrow uses (base typography, third-party integration) but not for component reuse.

questions.explanation: Components over @apply. Tailwind team explicitly recommends.

questions.prompt: Component takes `className` prop; user's `px-8` doesn't override internal `px-4`. Cause and fix?

choices.text: Both `px-4` and `px-8` ship in the final CSS at equal specificity; CSS source order decides which wins, not className string order. Fix: use `tailwind-merge` (`cn = (...args) => twMerge(clsx(args))`). twMerge knows Tailwind's utility groups and resolves conflicts — when two `px-*` classes appear, the last one in the string wins predictably. Every shadcn/ui component uses this pattern; standard for component libraries that accept className.

questions.explanation: Use tailwind-merge (cn helper). Resolves utility conflicts so last-listed class wins.

choices.text: Runtime cost from injecting styles at render, SSR setup complications, bundle size overhead (12-15KB), and critically — most CSS-in-JS libraries don't work with React Server Components (they need a runtime that doesn't fit RSC's serialization). As Next.js App Router and RSC became dominant, CSS-in-JS rapidly lost ground. Tailwind generates static CSS at build time (zero runtime, perfect RSC compatibility) so it became the natural successor.

questions.explanation: Runtime cost + SSR pain + RSC incompatibility = CSS-in-JS fell. Tailwind's static CSS fits RSC perfectly.
